source: Client avoided-ingredient text and ministry ingredient strings are compared through unsigned TSV synonym rows.
closest_control: Target policy conflicts: AGENTS.md and synonyms.tsv require unsigned rows to remain blocking, while spec 005 says only signed bindings may block.
sink: AllergyChecker.canBlock authors a server BLOCKED verdict from that unrestricted comparison.
impact: If spec 005 is authoritative, an unreviewed identity can suppress medicine; if AGENTS.md and the dictionary contract are authoritative, the behavior is intentional safety policy.
preconditions: An unsigned row participates in comparison and is medically wrong or insufficiently reviewed.
A human policy owner resolves the contradictory target documents in favor of signed-only blocking.
counterevidence: AGENTS.md §2-6 says unsigned rows stay blocked under AR-02.
synonyms.tsv explicitly says unsigned rows still block on purpose to avoid false negatives.
proof_gaps: Human and clinical owners must resolve which contradictory target policy is authoritative before this can be classified as a §2 bypass.

evidence.claim: The repository invariant says unsigned rows remain blocked under AR-02.
The dictionary comments say unsigned rows still block on purpose, and unsigned production rows exist.
Comparison uses the broad map irrespective of reviewer.
The medical-profile spec says only signed bindings may block.

affected_locations.detail: Five current aliases carry reviewer TODO while the file states that rows authorize a medicine-blocking match.
affected_locations.label: unreviewed_source

affected_locations.detail: Every alias is inserted into the authoritative synonyms map before isSigned only records a second reviewed map.
affected_locations.label: broken_review_gate

affected_locations.detail: Treats every returned SYNONYM as canBlock and removes/marks the medicine.
affected_locations.label: sink

affected_locations.label: root_control

affected_locations.detail: Builds the trusted retrieved ingredient set with normalizeIdentity.

affected_locations.detail: Normalizes model-authored ingredients the same way and accepts set equality.

affected_locations.label: sink

affected_locations.detail: The reviewer field is TODO for apap -> acetaminophen.
affected_locations.label: concrete_implementation

affected_locations.detail: All aliases enter synonyms before the reviewer check.
affected_locations.label: root_control

affected_locations.detail: The unrestricted alias map authors block-capable match confidence.
affected_locations.label: sink

affected_locations.label: concrete_implementation

affected_locations.detail: The salt-form alias enters the blocking map without signature.
affected_locations.label: root_control

affected_locations.detail: compare turns the unreviewed relation into block-capable confidence.
affected_locations.label: sink

affected_locations.label: concrete_implementation

affected_locations.detail: The salt alias is inserted into the unrestricted map before review state is applied.

affected_locations.detail: The unrestricted map determines blocking match confidence.

fix_direction: Require a human and clinical policy decision reconciling AGENTS.md, the dictionary contract, and spec 005 before changing blocking behavior.
rationale: The implementation is deterministic, but contradictory target policy prevents a confident verdict that unsigned blocking is a §2 bypass.

source: Anonymous GET /api/v1/drugs controls raw exclude_ingredients.
closest_control: DrugController silently drops values IngredientNormalizer cannot bind.
sink: Search can return no_match_found after a declared exclusion was removed.
impact: An official-looking no-match verdict is computed from an incomplete allergy set, bypassing §2-2.
preconditions: A syntactically accepted exclusion is unbound and the result contains the intended allergen.
counterevidence: The first-party picker normally sends reviewed keys, but this endpoint is public.

evidence.claim: Parenthesized input can produce no key.
An empty avoided set yields no_match_found.

affected_locations.detail: The anonymous search route accepts raw exclude_ingredients and forwards only normalizeAvoided output.
affected_locations.label: entrypoint

affected_locations.detail: A null normalization key is omitted without an error, warning, or unverified-allergen representation.
affected_locations.label: root_control

affected_locations.detail: If every exclusion was omitted, the checker returns no_match_found because it sees an empty avoided set.
affected_locations.label: broken_safety_default

affected_locations.detail: Search summaries are returned with allergy verdicts computed from the silently reduced set.
affected_locations.label: sink

fix_direction: Reject or mark incomplete every unbound exclusion; §2-2 requires human review.
rationale: Immutable target source deterministically drops unbound allergy exclusions before checking, so a declared exclusion can become no_match_found; no mutable probe citation is required.

source: Anonymous drug detail controls raw exclude_ingredients.
closest_control: The detail route applies the same silent normalization filter.
sink: A specific medicine card can return no_match_found after an exclusion was removed.
impact: The detail-route behavior is the same underlying exclusion-dropping defect already represented by R01-CAN-046, not an independent security finding.
preconditions: An accepted exclusion is unbound and the detail product contains the intended allergen.
counterevidence: Authoritative ministry ingredients cannot recover a declaration removed before comparison.
Search and detail routes share the same binding/drop mechanism and safety consequence.
Reporting both rows would double-count one root cause.

evidence.claim: Only surviving avoided keys reach the detail check.

affected_locations.detail: The independently reachable detail route accepts raw exclusions and sends only normalized survivors to DrugService.

affected_locations.label: broken_safety_default

affected_locations.label: sink

fix_direction: Carry the detail-route affected location and verification case under R01-CAN-046's remediation.
rationale: The detail route is a duplicate manifestation of R01-CAN-046's exclusion-dropping root cause, with the same safety impact and remediation.

source: A same-origin script, extension, device-local actor, or stale build writes same-version JSON-valid malformed answer data to sessionStorage.
closest_control: Version/JSON/outer-role checks do not runtime-validate nested MermAidAnswer fields.
sink: ChatScreen dereferences answer and drug fields without an error boundary.
impact: Malformed same-origin session state can disrupt only the same browser context whose writer already has equivalent UI disruption authority.
preconditions: Same-origin/sessionStorage write capability or stale same-version data exists; cross-origin pages alone cannot write it.
counterevidence: Current server responses are validated and sessionStorage dies with the tab.
The canonical affected locations are confined to a quarantined worktree and do not validate this exact target row.
The target-native analogous same-origin crash is already represented by R01-CAN-134.
No cross-user or privilege-boundary reach is shown.

evidence.claim: Same-version chat lacks a shape validator.
Restore checks syntax, not nested structure.
No error boundary contains the crash.

affected_locations.label: incomplete_restore_control

affected_locations.detail: JSON.parse output is cast to Partial<MermAidAnswer>; defaults cover absent/null fields but truthy wrong-typed drugs, uiActions, summary, and nested objects are preserved without validation.

affected_locations.detail: The component calls array methods and dereferences drug/action objects; for example drugs:[null] throws while reading drug.id.
affected_locations.label: render_sink

affected_locations.detail: No error boundary contains the restored-answer render failure.
affected_locations.label: uncontained_application_root

fix_direction: Treat defensive restore validation as reliability hardening under the target-native same-origin crash finding, not as a separate security issue.
rationale: The exact canonical authority is quarantined, while the target-native analogue is same-origin reliability already represented elsewhere and shows no distinct security boundary.

source: An actor with same-origin storage write capability stores a complete forged v2 medical answer.
closest_control: Schema version, JSON syntax, and shallow role checks provide no authenticity or re-grounding.
sink: ChatScreen and DrugCard render forged treatment, allergy state, prescription status, and source provenance.
impact: A same-origin writer can alter only its own browser's restored UI and already has equivalent ability to render misleading content directly; no added cross-user or privilege-boundary reach is shown.
preconditions: A same-origin script, extension, device-local actor, XSS, or top-level third-party script can write storage.
counterevidence: Tab-scoped lifetime and missing-source suppression reduce exposure; a complete forged sourceRef passes.
Cross-origin attackers cannot write this sessionStorage state.
R01-CAN-058 separately covers the conditional third-party SDK capability.
Dynamic browser proof was not preserved as immutable scan evidence.

evidence.claim: Stored answers have no integrity control.
Safety and verified-source UI trust restored fields.

affected_locations.detail: Any same-version assistant string from sessionStorage is JSON-parsed and passed to parseAnswer on reload; there is no integrity or provenance check.
affected_locations.label: entrypoint

affected_locations.detail: parseAnswer fills defaults but accepts supplied summary, urgency, drugs, sourceRefs, warnings and dataStatus as if the backend had already validated them.
affected_locations.label: root_control

affected_locations.detail: The tracked target retains the same stored-answer trust assumption under chatSession.v2.
affected_locations.label: target_relevance

affected_locations.detail: Restored fields render as primary medical summary, emergency state, warning and drug cards; a supplied non-fixture dataStatus avoids the fixture caveat.
affected_locations.label: sink

fix_direction: Keep restore integrity as defense-in-depth; track conditional third-party SDK compromise under R01-CAN-058 and same-origin storage reliability under R01-CAN-134/R01-CAN-135.
rationale: A same-origin writer gains no additional privilege by forging its own restored UI; conditional third-party capability is retained separately as R01-CAN-058.

source: A compromised Naver SDK/delivery path executes in the top-level application origin when Map opens.
closest_control: The URL is fixed HTTPS and map-triggered, but the script has ambient origin storage authority.
sink: Third-party code can read chat session, deviceId, and allergy preferences and can write forged answers.
impact: A vendor compromise could disclose consultation/bearer data and enable medical-answer tampering.
preconditions: The vendor or delivery chain is compromised and the user opens Map.
counterevidence: No evidence shows the current Naver SDK is malicious; loading is limited to the map path.
proof_gaps: Whether iframe/origin isolation is compatible with product requirements needs human architecture review.

evidence.claim: Chat and Map are mounted in the same top-level application origin.

affected_locations.detail: The page origin holds a persistent deviceId, consultation messages/session allergies in sessionStorage, and opt-in allergy preferences in localStorage.
affected_locations.label: source

affected_locations.detail: Opening a map appends a third-party script element directly to document.head; it is not isolated in a sandboxed/cross-origin iframe.
affected_locations.label: root_control

affected_locations.detail: The fetched SDK runs in the application document and receives normal page JavaScript authority before the map is marked ready.
affected_locations.label: sink

affected_locations.detail: Chat and Map remain mounted in the same top-level document and the SDK loads when the Map tab becomes active.
affected_locations.label: concrete_implementation

fix_direction: Evaluate isolated origin/iframe, strict CSP, and minimizing bearer data in the map origin.
rationale: Target source proves the externally loaded map SDK executes in the same origin as sensitive client state, but exploitability depends on vendor compromise or a human architecture decision, so needs_review remains appropriate.

source: Anonymous POST /profiles/{deviceId}/allergies supplies the bearer-like deviceId.
closest_control: The client response is generic, but ProfileService embeds deviceId in ApiException and the handler logs it.

impact: Log readers can reuse it to read or mutate consented allergy, favorite, alias, and memo state.
preconditions: The profile is not opted in so the exception fires, and a log reader obtains the record.
counterevidence: The browser error is redacted and the identifier was high entropy before logging.

source: The outbound hospital URI contains serviceKey and a remote failure creates a WebClient exception.
closest_control: Pinned Spring 6.2.19 request descriptions omit query strings; target code never logs getUri().
sink: No standard target log sink serializes the serviceKey query.
impact: No current target service-key disclosure; coordinate logging is separate.
preconditions: A future/nonstandard serializer would have to include query data or code would have to log getUri().
counterevidence: An isolated fake-upstream run sent the key upstream but found it absent from logs and client output.
proof_gaps: Future library/deployment logging behavior remains outside the immutable target.

source: Anonymous profile JSON contains over-limit allergy, alias, memo, or country text with line breaks.
closest_control: The response reduces errors to a field name, but invalid() first logs MethodArgumentNotValidException.getMessage().
sink: Spring FieldError rejectedValue enters warning logs unchanged.
impact: Sensitive profile text persists and attacker line breaks can forge log records.
preconditions: A bean-validation bound fails and the appender preserves formatted-message newlines.
counterevidence: Field bounds cap volume and client responses are sanitized.

source: Anonymous drug detail controls an unconstrained itemSeq path string, including percent-decoded separators.
closest_control: SLF4J parameterization does not neutralize CR/LF in formatted values.
sink: NotFoundException embeds itemSeq and its handler logs the message.
impact: An attacker can forge physical warning-log records and corrupt audit tooling.
preconditions: The lookup returns no product and Tomcat/appender preserve a decoded line separator.
counterevidence: Request-target limits bound volume; fixture detail returns a product and misses this sink.
proof_gaps: No full HTTP live/no-result reproduction was run; pinned Tomcat source supports acceptance.

source: Chat users supply medicine/product text and the model derives ingredient terms from health context.
closest_control: Bounds prevent control-character injection but accepted terms are not redacted.
sink: DrugContextRetriever logs ingredient/product terms at INFO.
impact: Exact user-authored product substrings and model-derived consultation terms persist in server logs, violating the §2-5 boundary that consultation transcripts never persist.
preconditions: Drug retrieval has accepted terms and INFO logs are retained/exported.
counterevidence: The full free-form symptom message is not logged on this path.
Logged terms are length- and count-bounded.

evidence.claim: Terms are bounded and product names origin-bound.

source: Anonymous public-upstream requests coincide with a peer that accepts then stalls response completion.
closest_control: Pinned Reactor Netty supplies finite default connect and pool-acquire timeouts, but the target sets no explicit response/read timeout or deadline for the whole operation.
sink: Mono.block and boundedElastic callers wait without cancellation.
impact: Stalls can retain connections, servlet requests, and workers until drug/facility/geocode care surfaces are denied.
preconditions: An upstream/network stalls and enough concurrent uncached requests arrive.
counterevidence: The runtime has finite default connect and pool-acquire timeouts.
Those defaults do not bound an accepted connection whose response stalls or never completes.

evidence.claim: The shared WebClient config sets no explicit response or read timeout.
Parallel async work has no whole-operation deadline.
A representative caller blocks on response completion without a per-call timeout.

affected_locations.detail: The public WebClient sets DNS resolution and a 4 MiB decode cap but no response, read, or whole-request timeout.
affected_locations.label: root_control

affected_locations.detail: A representative anonymous route synchronously reaches the public client after only a 200-character query bound.
affected_locations.label: entrypoint

affected_locations.detail: The request ends in block(); fallback/error handling runs only after the reactive operation terminates with an error.
affected_locations.label: sink

affected_locations.detail: Hospital paging repeatedly invokes bodyToMono(...).block() without a deadline.
affected_locations.label: sink

affected_locations.detail: Fan-out parks blocking calls on the shared boundedElastic scheduler and waits for all results, so stalled responses retain shared workers.
affected_locations.label: concrete_implementation

affected_locations.detail: LLM operations apply explicit whole-Mono timeouts, demonstrating that the shared HttpClient alone does not supply the missing public-call deadline.
affected_locations.label: counterexample

affected_locations.detail: No transport timeout.
affected_locations.label: missing control

affected_locations.detail: Unbounded block.
affected_locations.label: representative sink

affected_locations.detail: Waits for multiple blocked calls.
affected_locations.label: parallel sink

fix_direction: Set explicit connect, response, read, and operation deadlines with truthful fallback/error handling.
rationale: The connect and pool-acquire phases are finitely bounded by runtime defaults, but an accepted incomplete response remains unbounded because the target sets no response/read or whole-operation deadline.

source: Anonymous facility/open_now requests trigger uncached pharmacy calls.

preconditions: Government pharmacy upstream accepts then stalls. Enough concurrent public requests reach an uncached path.
counterevidence: Fixture/hybrid fallback waits for an error and cannot cancel a pending call.
Reporting each blocking callsite separately would double-count the same shared configuration root cause.

evidence.claim: The target calls bodyToMono(...).block() without timeout.
The shared client has no deadline.
